ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken Kheema Pav. Mapped to the broad 'chicken dish' class (FOODON_03315702) as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for spiced minced chicken curry with bread is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, minced — used as the primary ingredient anchor for the kheema component. Pav bread component is a wheat-flour soft roll; no combined FoodEx2 composite code exists for this dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for Chicken Kheema Pav. A0CKN (minced chicken) is used as the dominant ingredient anchor. The pav bread component is acknowledged but lacks a separate FoodEx2 composite entry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Chicken Kheema Pav or a sufficiently close analogue in the clinical food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one plate comprising approximately 250–280 g of spiced minced chicken kheema curry accompanied by two soft pav bread rolls (approximately 170–200 g combined), totalling roughly 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) — Chicken Mince / Keema and Wheat Bread composite estimate; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for ground chicken (NDB 05298) and white dinner roll (NDB 18350)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Kheema Pav as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite: approximately 60% cooked spiced minced chicken (ICMR-NIN chicken keema / USDA ground chicken cooked) and 40% pav bread (USDA white dinner roll / soft roll). The input record's per-100g values (energy 151 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 7.78 g, carbohydrate 10.67 g) are consistent with this composite and have been retained with minor rounding adjustments. Sodium 266.7 mg/100g is plausible given added salt in kheema and butter on pav. All values cross-checked against ICMR-NIN Table 1 (cooked chicken dishes) and USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283 — White bread / soft white roll GI ~70–75; mixed meat-and-bread dish estimated at GI ~55–65 based on protein and fat attenuation of glycaemic response.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Kheema Pav as a composite dish. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on: (1) pav bread (soft white roll) GI ~70–75 per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) protein and fat content of the kheema component (10 g protein/100g, 7.8 g fat/100g) attenuate the glycaemic response by approximately 10–15 GI units per established mixed-meal methodology (Brand-Miller et al.). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 60 × carbohydrate per serving (48.15 g from 450 g × 10.7 g/100g) / 100 = 28.9, rounded to 28.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.7 mg/100g; estimated 1200 mg per full 450 g serving
hypertension.guidance: At 266.7 mg sodium per 100 g, a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium — over half the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertensive individuals — so portion should be limited to half a serving and pav should be requested without added butter or salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined white flour pav drives elevated glycaemic load (GL ~29 per serving); low dietary fibre 1.1 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The pav bread component is made from refined white flour (GI ~70–75) and contributes a glycaemic load of approximately 29 per full serving, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one pav, request whole-wheat pav where available, and increase the kheema-to-bread ratio to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate balance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.3 g/100g (14.85 g per serving); sodium 266.7 mg/100g; cholesterol 40 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A full 450 g serving provides approximately 14.9 g saturated fat — approaching the 20 g daily upper limit for cardiovascular risk management — and 1200 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit to a half-serving, request minimal butter on pav, and avoid pairing with high-sodium chutneys or fried accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic, cumin, garam masala) present in material quantities; saturated fat below 5 g/100g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The kheema spice blend provides clinically relevant anti-inflammatory compounds (curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from ginger, allicin from garlic); however, individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor response to tomatoes and green chillies, and should request whole-wheat pav with minimal butter to reduce refined-carbohydrate-driven systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Kheema Pav is a popular Indian street-food and restaurant dish consisting of spiced minced chicken (kheema) curry served alongside two soft, lightly buttered pav bread rolls. The kheema is prepared by sautéing finely minced chicken with onions, ginger-garlic paste, green chillies, tomatoes, and a blend of aromatic spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and garam masala, then simmering until the mixture forms a thick, fragrant, semi-dry curry. The pav rolls are soft white-flour buns, typically pan-toasted with butter, providing a pillowy, slightly crisp contrast to the richly spiced mince. The dish originates from the Indian subcontinent and is widely served in Indian restaurants and food courts across Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 151 kcal, 10 g protein, 7.8 g fat (3.3 g saturated), and 10.7 g carbohydrate with only 1.1 g dietary fibre, reflecting the low-fibre refined flour pav component. Sodium is elevated at 266.7 mg/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 60 (medium) due to the white flour pav, attenuated by the protein and fat of the kheema. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (high sodium per serving), moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes (elevated glycaemic load ~29 per full serving from refined pav), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium). The anti-inflammatory spice profile makes it suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients who tolerate nightshades. Halal-suitable when prepared with certified chicken and dairy-free butter alternatives.
common_user_queries: Is chicken kheema pav good for diabetics?
Chicken mince curry with bread calories
Can I eat kheema pav if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Kheema Pav as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN chicken keema data and USDA SR Legacy soft white roll data, cross-validated against the input record's per-100g values which are internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white bread values attenuated by mixed-meal protein and fat. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish match exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chicken Kheema Pav as a composite dish is not available in ICMR-NIN (2017) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation; only total fat and saturated fat are reported. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy at the composite level; estimated ~200 mg/100g from onions, tomatoes, and chicken but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN (2017) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the composite dish level as of 2026-04-16. 4. trans_fat_g 0.1 g/100g is retained from the input record; this is plausible from butter used on pav and is consistent with USDA SR Legacy values for buttered white rolls. 5. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Chicken Kheema Pav does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is assumed based on typical restaurant preparation with chicken; individual vendor certification should be verified. 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not on the approved source list; ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central were used instead.
